does: This paper introduces CEDAR, a post-hoc method for interpreting vision-language model embeddings by learning an invertible transformation with a top-k sparsity bottleneck that maps entangled representations into sparse, axis-aligned disentangled coordinates without expanding dimensionality. The method extracts interpretable features where individual coordinates correspond to human-understandable concepts—textual labels in CLIP-like models and natural language descriptions in generative models like BLIP. Through experiments on vision-language datasets, CEDAR demonstrates competitive reconstruction-sparsity trade-offs while producing explanations more aligned with human perception than prior approaches, revealing that apparent entanglement in embeddings may be decomposable into underlying compositional structure.
connects: This work builds on the emerging literature of mechanistic interpretability for deep neural networks, particularly extending sparse autoencoders (SAEs) which extract interpretable features but require dimensionality expansion. CEDAR responds to a key limitation of SAEs—their compromise of original geometric structure—by adapting techniques from disentangled representation learning that aim to separate independent factors of variation. The paper connects to prior work on understanding vision-language models like CLIP and BLIP, whose multimodal embeddings have shown surprising capabilities but remain largely uninterpretable. It also draws on the broader tradition of concept-based explanations and compositional semantics in neural networks, combining invertible transformations with sparsity constraints to maintain embedding geometry while achieving interpretability.
enables: This work opens avenues for more transparent deployment and auditing of vision-language models by making their internal semantic structure explicit and verifiable. The axis-aligned disentangled coordinates could enable targeted model editing, fine-tuning, or filtering by manipulating specific semantic dimensions. Future work could apply CEDAR to larger models, investigate whether disentangled coordinates reveal biases or failure modes, or use the discovered compositional structure for improved zero-shot generalization and transfer learning. The method also points toward developing sparsity-based interpretability tools that preserve embedding geometry, potentially applicable to other multimodal or unimodal architectures beyond vision-language models.
